solutions.solution:
The oil glut may be the remote cause of fire but in our practical context, we mean the immediate and the proximate cause. Here, the immediate cause of fire is the policy-holder igniting it and this is a proximate cause.

Principle:
We have seen that an effect takes place because of several necessary conditions, some fulfilled. One of these several necessary conditions, going missing because it makes little difference between the occurrence and non-occurrence at the result. In our example of the policy-holder igniting, kerosene was always there in the atmosphere and kerosene oil and matchbox were available in the market. What made the big difference was the fact that the policy-holder sprayed the kerosene and ignited the fire. Therefore, here the cigarette because the fire was the policy-holder igniting the fire. As a matter of fact, imagine that India and Pakistan sign a peace treaty of mutual non-interference and non-abetment to cross-border terrorism. As a result, very few incidents, militants crossing over from the border take place. Now, here, the fact that few militants are crossing over may also be caused by factors such as expected ruthless winter, vigilant patrolling, dying support for militancy etc. But these are some were always there. What perhaps made the difference was the treaty. In the absence of cross-border terrorism and, therefore, this should be considered as principal cause. As another example, imagine that Sudha has very keen interest in learning computers. This effect may be caused by several things. For example, one reason could be that computers are cheap. So, Sudha bought a computer easily. Another reason could be that Sudha is being taught by a very good teacher. And then, another reason could be that Sudha has a natural aptitude for computers. Now, here, had Sudha not had a natural aptitude for computers or a good teacher wouldn't have made much difference. Thus, although the latter are also causes in their own right, the principal cause is her own natural aptitude for computers due to the reason that makes the big difference between her interest and non-interest.

Note 1: Difference between immediate and principal cause
Although they sound similar there is a difference between immediate cause and principal cause. Immediate causes are the most proximate in time, to the effect. The principal cause is the main and the most important reason for the effect. [You have read Indian history you may appreciate that the principal cause of the mutiny of 1857 was the Doctrine of Lapse and other repressive policies of Lord Dalhousie but the immediate cause was the fact that the sepoys were unwilling to bite cartridges that had fat of sacred animals such as cows.]

[Note 2: In our examinations, however, we are asked to find out if a cause is immediate as well as principal. Roughly, it means that the said cause must be the principal reason of the given effect and it should also be fairly proximate in time to the said effect.]

Our strategy for solution includes a 'Quick Check' to eliminate obviously wrong choices. The quick check includes: 'Cause always antecedent' - quick check ensures the cause will always occur before the effect. Hence, if two events are given, then the effect that is chronologically antecedent to the other can't be regarded as a possible cause. In other words, we can look for ways of checking which of the two events occurred first. (For example, if we can check the tense etc. of the sentences.) Similarly, consider Q.2 of our simple problem. Here, event A has occurred today, and event B took place last week. This means that A occurs before B and hence even if B is possible cause, B hence is a cause. This eliminates choice 1.3. Again, event A is an event which has already happened. Here, event A is a past event and the event (fall in prices) has already happened. While event B is still happening. For the same reasons, B can't be a cause. This eliminates choices 1.3. For the same reasons, B can't be a cause of A and the simple problem and thus eliminates choices 1 and 3.
